texts.label: text
texts.orig: The International Energy Agency reported that the industrial processes (non-energy sector) were responsible for 7% of the global greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in 2016 [1]. From the Canadian standpoint, the Environment and Climate Change Canada disclosed that industrial processes and product use contributed to 7.53% of the 2016 GHG emissions by IPCC sector [2]. However, carbon dioxide (CO2) was discovered to be responsible for 79% of Canada's total GHG emissions [2]. The non-energy industrial processes and product use are made up of but not limited to the chemical industries, metal production and production of mineral products etc. A key process evident in some nonenergy industrial processes is the production of quicklime (calcium oxide, CaO) from limestone calcination in a lime kiln. Eq. (1) shows a typical limestone calcination reaction to produce quicklime which also generates CO2. Previous studies have shown that the CO2 concentration in the exhaust gas stream exiting the lime kiln range from 22% to 29 %

texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This experimental study covered pilot plant analysis of novel AMP and 1,5-diamino-2-methylpentane (DA2MP) amine solvent blend for CO2 capture from a lime kiln. The gas flow rate (F(GAS)), amine solution flow rate (F(amine)), CO2 concentration and reboiler temperature (T(REB)) were kept at 14 SLPM, 50 mL/min, 30 vol.% CO2 (N2 balance) and 120 °C respectively. The AMP concentration was kept at 2 kmol/m 3 while DA2MP was varied from 1.5 kmol/m 3 to 2 kmol/m 3 . The MEA and AMP-DA2MP comparative analysis was based on rich amine loading (αrich, mol CO2/mol amine), lean amine loading (αlean, mol CO2/mol amine), cyclic loading (CL, mol CO2/mol amine), cyclic capacity (CC, mol CO2/L-amine soln.), CO2 absorption rate (rabs, g-CO2/hr), CO2 absorption efficiency (%), regeneration energy (Qreg, GJ/tonne CO2), absorber overall average volumetric mass transfer coefficient (KGav(ave), kmol/kPa.hr. m 3 ), desorber mass transfer coefficient (KLav, hr -1 ), initial amine solution utilized (Aminesoln._utilized, g-amine soln./g-CO2) and initial amine solution cost (US$/g-CO2). The influence of sensible energy (Qsen, GJ/tonne CO2), vaporization energy (Qvap, GJ/tonne CO2), and desorption heat (ΔHdes, GJ/tonne CO2) towards regeneration energy (Qreg) was also examined. Results showed that the AMPDA2MP blend possesses higher CO2 absorption efficiency (up to 36.17%), higher KGav(ave) (up to 65.85%), higher KLav (up to 28.29%) and lower Qreg (up to 32.54%) compared to the single solvent MEA. Also, MEA possessed higher initial amine solution utilized (28.86%) and lower initial amine solution cost (28.5%) compared to the AMP-DA2MP blend. This is an initial revelation that AMP-DA2MP can provide cost-effective CO2 capture from a lime kiln.

The MEA and AMP-DA2MP comparative analysis was based on rich amine loading (αrich, mol CO2/mol amine), lean amine loading (αlean, mol CO2/mol amine), cyclic loading (CL, mol CO2/mol amine), cyclic capacity (CC, mol CO2/L-amine soln.), CO2 absorption rate (rabs, g-CO2/hr), CO2 absorption efficiency (%), regeneration energy (Qreg, GJ/tonne CO2), absorber overall average volumetric mass transfer coefficient (KGav(ave), kmol/kPa.hr. m 3 ), desorber mass transfer coefficient (KLav, hr -1 ), initial amine solution utilized (Aminesoln._utilized, g-amine soln./g-CO2) and initial amine solution cost (US$/g-CO2). texts.orig: (CO2 and N2) from the fuel firing to generate the total exhaust gas from the lime kiln. The CO2 emissions produced from the lime kiln are both from the limestone which is predominantly calcium carbonate (CaCO3) and the fuel used for kiln direct firing. Currently, CO2 emission from the limestone cannot be reduced because of the stoichiometry of the limestone calcination reaction (Eq. (1)). Therefore, there is a need for capturing the CO2 from the lime kiln process. From Fig. 1 it can be seen that the product of the lime kiln (clinker) which is at very high temperature is sent to the clinker primary cooler (P-Cooler) to reduce the temperature and then to the secondary cooler (S-Cooler) to further drop the temperature to maximum 100 °C. The very hot clinker presents a potential source of energy and heat for amine solution regeneration in CO2 capture plants which can make the CO2 capture process from a lime kiln 'zero parasitic energy'.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Absorption, adsorption, membrane, cryogenic and biological process are technologies available for CO2 capture. However, CO2 capture using reactive amine-based solvents are the most studied, matured and has the capability of 90% CO2 capture [8-11]. Primary amine (monoethanolamine, MEA), secondary amine (diethanolamine, DEA) and tertiary amines (methyldiethanolamine, MDEA) are the major classifications of amine solvents. Sterically hindered amine (2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol, AMP) and polyamines (piperazine, PZ; diethylenetriamine, DETA) are other classifications of amine solvents. The concentration of the standard and base case amine solvent is the single solvent primary amine, 5 kmol/m 3 MEA (30 wt.% MEA). Previous studies have reported CO2 capture from the lime kiln of cement plants using MEA [4,6,12]. The application of MEA is because of its low cost, high absorber mass transfer and CO2 absorption rate, however, its major disadvantages are high regeneration energy, degradation, corrosion and vaporization [13-18]. This prompted the investigation of blended amine solvents, and previous and recent studies have reported improvement in the CO2 capture capability of amine blends compared to single solvents [7,19-24]. Part of the knowledge gap is firstly lack of substantial published experimental research on CO2 capture from the lime kiln, and secondly to develop energy efficient and cost effective novel amine solvent blends for CO2 capture from lime kilns.

Five thermocouples inserted at different positions along the absorber column and connected to data acquisition system (Labview, National Instruments, Product of Hungary) provides realtime monitoring and recording of the temperature profile. The F(GAS), CO2 concentration, F(amine), and T(REB) of 14 SLPM, 30 vol.% CO2 (with N2 balance), 50 mL/min and 120 °C respectively. Apart from the standard MEA (5 kmol/m 3 MEA), the amine blend concentration was varied from 2 kmol/m 3 AMP-1.5 kmol/m 3 DA2MP and 2kmol/m 3 AMP-2 kmol/m 3 DA2MP. The simulated flue gas operating pressure entering the absorber bottom is from 1.01 bar to 1.03 bar. The water saturator located upstream of the absorber column bottom (Fig. 2) allows the simulated flue gas to be saturated with water at the delivery temperature (20 °C to 21 °C), thereby making the flue gas a mixture of CO2+N2 + H2O. This delivery temperature means that the water vapor in the flue gas entering the absorber bottom section will not be sufficient enough to dilute or reduce the concentration of the aqueous amine solution entering from the absorber top section. Before commencing the absorption experiment, the variable area flow meter (0 to 150 mL/min range, Cole-Parmer, Model: TMR1-010,102) was calibrated to the desired amine flow rate. The digital gear pump purchased from Cole-Parmer USA (Model: 75,211-30) was used to pump the CO2 lean amine solution to the top of the absorber.

texts.orig: Steady state is usually confirmed in two ways, firstly when the CO2 concentration at the top exit of the absorber does not change ( ± 0.1 vol. %), and secondly when the temperature profiles changes negligibly ( ± 1 °C) within 5 min. Typically, steady state is reached at maximum 25min, and at this point, the amine loading is analyzed and temperature profiles recorded followed by analyzing the CO2 concentration profile. An infrared (IR) CO2 analyzer supplied by Quantek Instruments, Grafton, MA, USA (Model 906; measures maximum 100 vol.% CO2; ± 2% accuracy) was used for analyzing the CO2 concentration profile.

The temperatures are measured, monitored realtime and recorded by thermocouples and Labview data acquisition system. After achieving steady state the hot CO2 lean amine solution is cooled and taken to the absorption column for the second cycle CO2 absorption. This enables the CO2 absorption to be conducted with the CO2 lean loading of the amine solution that is peculiar to the regeneration temperature.

For the desorber column, the mass transfer is determined from the liquid side. Mass transfer in the desorber column takes into account the movement of species of interest from the liquid side (CO2 loaded amine solution) to the vapor side through the interface of the liquid-gas. texts.orig: Fig. 4 reveals the comparative analysis of the lean amine loading (αlean) and rich amine loading (αrich) of MEA and AMP-DA2MP blend. It was discovered that the lean amine loading of the AMP-DA2MP blend is 25.13%-28.2% higher than that of single solvent MEA. Considering that DA2MP is a primary diamine solvent, several carbamate species are expected to be present in the CO2 loaded amine solution. The expected DA2MP carbamate species are DA2MP carbamate (DA2MPCOO-), protonated carbamate (H + DA2MPCOO-), and DA2MP dicarbamate (DA2MP(COO-)2), and in addition AMP carbamate (AMPCOO-) is expected to be present in small concentration. Not all carbamate species will break down during the regeneration process, thereby leading to higher lean amine loading.

texts.orig: The cyclic loading (CL), cyclic capacity (CC) and CO2 absorption rate (rabs) of MEA and AMP-DA2MP are shown in Fig. 5. It was discovered that the cyclic loading of the AMP-DA2MP blend is 57.37% and 63.16% higher than that of MEA. Though the lean amine loading of the amine blend is higher than that of MEA, the much higher rich amine loading is the reason for the higher cyclic loading of the AMP-DA2MP blend (Figs. 4 and 5). At a constant concentration of AMP (2 kmol/m 3 ), as the DA2MP concentration is increased from 1.5 kmol/m 3 to 2 kmol/m 3 the cyclic loading slightly decreased by 3.55%. This is because of the more significant reduction in the rich amine loading due to higher amine concentration (Figs. 4 and 5).

texts.orig: The cyclic capacity of AMP-DA2MP blend can be seen to be up to 25.9% higher compared to the single solvent MEA (Fig. 5). This increase in cyclic capacity is not as significant as the increase observed in the cyclic loading. It can be attributed to the higher amine concentration of MEA (5 kmol/m 3 ) compared to the AMP-DA2MP blend (3.5 kmol/m 3 to 4 kmol/m 3 ). The effect of total amine concentration on the cyclic capacity is the reason for the higher cyclic capacity as the total AMP-DA2MP concentration increased from 3.5 kmol/m 3 to 4 kmol/m 3 (Fig. 5).

texts.orig: The trend in the rate of CO2 absorption can be explained with the temperature profile of the absorber (Fig. 6). The previous study reported the relationship between amine reactivity and absorber temperature profile [41]. They reported that higher absorber temperature profile is an indication of the amine reactivity with CO2. Fig. 6 shows that the absorber temperature profile and maximum temperature (Tmaxabs) followed the trend 5 kmol/m 3 MEA < 2kmol/m 3 AMP-1.5 kmol/ m 3 DA2MP < 2kmol/m 3 AMP-2kmol/m 3 DA2MP which is the same trend as the CO2 absorption rate. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 absorption efficiency of MEA and AMP-DA2MP is displayed in Fig. 7. Results showed that the CO2 absorption efficiency of the AMPDA2MP blend is 17.85% and 36.17% higher than the MEA solvent. This is due to the presence of the two primary amino groups in the DA2MP solvent which is believed increased the reaction of AMP-DA2MP with CO2. The absorber Tmax-abs as shown in Fig. 6 also reveals that the higher temperature experienced by the AMP-DA2MP contributed to the CO2 absorption efficiency. Also, in order to achieve the same CO2 absorption efficiency, results indicated that the AMP-DA2MP blend will require reduced amine flow rate. The absorber Tmax-abs as shown in Fig. 6 also reveals that the higher temperature experienced by the AMP-DA2MP contributed to the CO2 absorption efficiency. Also, in order to achieve the same CO2 absorption efficiency, results indicated that the AMP-DA2MP blend will require reduced amine flow rate. Reduction in amine flow rate translates to reduced capital costs and operating costs. The capital cost will be reduced due to reductions in column diameter, and pump size while

texts.orig: The mass transfer coefficient of the absorber and desorber are important parameters because they provide an indication of the height requirement of the absorber and desorber. High absorber mass transfer coefficient results to lower absorber height and same trend applies to the desorber. Lower absorber and desorber heights will lead to reduced capital and operating costs. The lower operating costs are because the rich amine solution pump and lean amine solution pump will require less electrical energy due to the reduced head. Fig. 8 displays the absorber overall average volumetric mass transfer coefficient (KGav(ave)) and desorber overall mass transfer coefficient (KLav).

texts.orig: 3.5. Regeneration energy

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 11 shows the comparative regeneration energy (Qreg) of MEA and the AMP-DA2MP blend. The individual contribution of sensible energy (Qsen, GJ/tonne CO2), desorption heat (ΔHdes, GJ/tonne CO2) and vaporization energy (Qvap, GJ/tonne CO2) to the regeneration energy is also depicted in Fig. 11. Pilot plant results showed that the regeneration energy of MEA is 22.29% and 32.54% higher than the AMPDA2MP blend. This is credited to the higher desorber temperature profile of the AMP-DA2MP blend (Fig. 10) which enhanced the interaction of species in the CO2 loaded amine solution, hence liberation of CO2 from the liquid phase to the vapor phase. Some species believed to be present in the CO2 loaded AMP-DA2MP blend are protonated AMP (AMPH + ), protonated DA2MP (DA2MPH + ), diprotonated DA2MP (DA2MPH 2+ 2 ), bicarbonate ion (HCO3-), AMP carbamate (AMPCOO-), DA2MP carbamate (DA2MPCOO-), protonated carbamate (H + DA2MPCOO-), and DA2MP dicarbamate (DA2MP(COO-)2). It has been reported that the AMPCOO- undergoes hydrolysis due to a steric hindrance to producing free AMP and HCO3-, thereby increasing the concentration of HCO3- and reducing that of AMPCOO- [42,43]. The contribution of HCO3towards reducing regeneration energy was previously stated by Shi et al. [44]. Also, both the branch chain and long carbon chain between the two primary amino groups of DA2MP is also believed will make the DA2MP carbamates unstable, hence making it easier to break down. This structural behavior is also expected to make the AMPH + , DA2MPH +, and DA2MPH 2+ 2 unstable, thereby releasing free amines and protons (H3O + or H + ). Singh and Versteeg reported to the effect of long carbon between two amino groups and branch chain towards increasing CO2 liberation from the liquid phase [45]. Eqs. (26)-(36) reveal possible reaction pathways of the species available in the AMP-DA2MP blend.

texts.orig: The lower regeneration energy of the AMP-DA2MP blend compared to MEA is mainly due to the substantial reduction in the desorption heat (21.59% and 63.88% lower) and then sensible energy (26.32% and 35.34% lower) as shown in Fig. 11. The lower sensible energy is because of the lower AMP-DA2MP blend mass flow rate and specific heat capacity compared to single solvent MEA (Eq. (22)). Though the water concentration of the AMP-DA2MP blend is lower than MEA, the vaporization energy of the AMP-DA2MP blend was discovered to be 11.11% and 66.11% higher than that of MEA. This is because of the higher temperature of the vapor stream exiting the top of the desorber which is as a result of higher desorber temperature profile (Fig. 10). In addition, the high CO2 production rate of the AMP-DA2MP blend, higher vapor pressure and low boiling point of AMP (compared to MEA) also contributed to the higher vaporization energy of AMP-DA2MP. The high CO2 production rate is believed to have led to the increased movement of H2O from the liquid phase to the vapor phase.

texts.orig: 3.6. Initial amine utilized and initial amine cost
texts.text: 3.6. Initial amine utilized and initial amine cost

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The amount of aqueous amine solution needed and the amine cost associated per CO2 captured is revealed by the initial amine solution utilized (g-amine soln./g-CO2) and initial amine cost (US$/g-CO2). The capital cost of a CO2 capture plant is affected by both the initial amine solution utilized and initial amine cost. This is because lower initial amine solution utilized translates to the reduced absorber and desorber diameters. On the other hand, a lower initial amine cost will decrease the cost required to buy the amine solution during the commissioning or start-up of the CO2 capture plant.

texts.orig: The initial amine solution utilized of MEA was discovered to be 17.31% and 28.86% higher than the AMP-DA2MP blend (Fig. 12). This is because of the lower mass flow rate and higher CO2 absorption rate of the AMP-DA2MP blend (Fig. 5). It is an early indication that the AMPDA2MP blend will require smaller absorber and desorber diameters compared to single solvent MEA. This underlines the importance of employing low-density amine solvents that also exhibit high CO2 absorption rate. However, the initial amine cost for the MEA solvent is 26% and 28.5% lower than the AMP-DA2MP blend (Fig. 12). This is due to the higher unit cost (Costamine, US$/g-amine solvent) of DA2MP and AMP compared to MEA. In general, the reduction in initial amine solution utilized as experienced by the AMP-DA2MP outweighs the increase in the initial amine solution cost when compared to MEA.

This was mainly contributed by the lower mass flow rate and higher CO2 absorption rate of 2 kmol/m 3 AMP-2kmol/m 3 DA2MP compared to 2 kmol/m 3 AMP-1.5 kmol/m 3 DA2MP (Figs. 5 and 9). On the other hand, the initial amine solution cost was observed to increase (2% increase) as the DA2MP concentration increased by 33.33% (from 1.5 kmol/m 3 to 2 kmol/m 3 ). The increase in the initial amine solution cost was not as significant as the increase in the DA2MP concentration because of the substantial increase in the CO2 absorption rate (Fig. 5).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pilot plant study of CO2 capture from lime kiln showed that the CO2 absorption efficiency, absorber overall average volumetric mass transfer, desorber overall mass transfer coefficient and initial amine solution utilized for the AMP-DA2MP blend were higher than that of MEA. This is a revelation that the height and diameter of the absorber and desorber will be smaller for the AMP-DA2MP blend compared to MEA, hence reduction in both capital and operating costs. The initial amine solution cost which also contributes to the capital cost was discovered to be higher for the AMP-DA2MP blend.
texts.text: Pilot plant study of CO2 capture from lime kiln showed that the CO2 absorption efficiency, absorber overall average volumetric mass transfer, desorber overall mass transfer coefficient and initial amine solution utilized for the AMP-DA2MP blend were higher than that of MEA. This is a revelation that the height and diameter of the absorber and desorber will be smaller for the AMP-DA2MP blend compared to MEA, hence reduction in both capital and operating costs. The initial amine solution cost which also contributes to the capital cost was discovered to be higher for the AMP-DA2MP blend.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Also, the regeneration energy which contributes significantly to the variable operating cost of CO2 capture plants was observed to be substantially lower for the AMP-DA2MP blend compared to MEA. This reduction in the regeneration was mainly due to the much lower sensible energy and desorption heat of the AMP-DA2MP blend. On the other hand, the higher exit temperature from the desorber top means that additional cooling water for the condenser will be needed for the AMP-DA2MP system.
texts.text: Also, the regeneration energy which contributes significantly to the variable operating cost of CO2 capture plants was observed to be substantially lower for the AMP-DA2MP blend compared to MEA. This reduction in the regeneration was mainly due to the much lower sensible energy and desorption heat of the AMP-DA2MP blend. On the other hand, the higher exit temperature from the desorber top means that additional cooling water for the condenser will be needed for the AMP-DA2MP system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In conclusion, cost-effective CO2 capture from the lime kiln is expected to be achieved by the novel AMP-DA2MP blend. This technology cuts across the cement manufacturing industry, aluminum industry, iron and steel industry, and pulp mills etc.
